posts.title: agents need plumbing not prompts
posts.hook: Everyone's talking about AI agents. Almost nobody's talking about the infrastructure that makes them actually work.
posts.body:
Everyone's talking about AI agents. Almost nobody's talking about the infrastructure that makes them actually work.

I run 6 automated agents in production right now. The prompt engineering took maybe 10% of the build time. The other 90%?

✅ Persistent state management so agents remember context across runs
✅ Error handling for when APIs flake at 2am
✅ Scheduling and orchestration so agents don't step on each other
✅ Logging that actually tells you what went wrong
✅ Cost tracking so you don't wake up to a $400 bill

The gap between "cool demo" and "runs in production every night without me touching it" is enormous. And it's not a prompt gap. It's a systems engineering gap.

Karpathy's right that we need better infrastructure for agentic systems. But I'd push further. The infrastructure isn't just cloud compute and model serving. It's the boring stuff. Cron jobs. Database writes. Retry logic. Health checks.

The builders who win with agents won't be the ones with the best prompts. They'll be the ones who treat agents like production software, not science experiments.

If your agent can't run for a week without you babysitting it, you don't have an agent. You have a demo.
posts.cta: What's the most unglamorous piece of your agent infrastructure? The thing that keeps it alive but nobody talks about?

remixes.label: war-stories-from-production
remixes.hook: My AI agent crashed at 3am last Tuesday. The fix had nothing to do with the prompt.
remixes.body:
My AI agent crashed at 3am last Tuesday. The fix had nothing to do with the prompt.

The Anthropic API returned a 529. My agent had no retry logic. It wrote a half-finished blog post to production, then died silently. No alert. No fallback. Just a broken page sitting there until I woke up.

That single failure taught me more about agent engineering than any prompt tutorial.

Here's what I rebuilt after that night:

A retry layer with exponential backoff and a circuit breaker. If the API fails 3 times, the agent skips the task and logs it for morning review instead of producing garbage output.

A state checkpoint system. Every agent writes its progress to disk before each major step. If it crashes mid-run, it picks up where it left off instead of starting over.

A dead man's switch. If an agent hasn't reported healthy in 60 minutes, I get a notification. No more silent failures.

None of this is sexy. None of it involves clever system prompts or chain-of-thought tricks. It's just plumbing. Logging. Error handling. The same boring infrastructure that makes any production system reliable.

The prompt is the easy part. Keeping the thing alive at 3am is the actual engineering.
remixes.cta: What's the worst silent failure you've had with an automated agent? How'd you catch it?

remixes.label: iceberg-analogy
remixes.hook: The prompt is the tip of the iceberg. Below the waterline is where agent engineering actually lives.
remixes.body:
The prompt is the tip of the iceberg. Below the waterline is where agent engineering actually lives.

I've been running production agents for months now. Six of them, handling everything from content generation to CRM enrichment to daily reporting. People always ask about the prompts. Nobody asks about the 14 other things that make them work.

Below the waterline:

Scheduling. Which agents run when. Dependencies between them. What happens when agent B needs output from agent A but agent A failed.

State. Agents need memory. Not just conversation history. Persistent state that tracks what they've done, what they haven't, and what changed since last run.

Observability. If you can't see what your agent did, you can't trust it. Every run gets logged. Every decision gets a paper trail.

Recovery. APIs go down. Rate limits hit. Data sources change formats. Each failure mode needs its own handling.

I'd estimate 80% of my agent codebase is infrastructure. The prompts themselves are maybe 200 lines total across all six agents. The plumbing around them is thousands.

If you're building agents and spending most of your time on prompts, you're optimizing the wrong layer.
remixes.cta: What percentage of your agent codebase is actually prompt vs. infrastructure? I'd bet the ratio surprises you.

If you're building AI agents for production use, I'd recommend spending 80% of your time on infrastructure and 20% on prompts. Most teams invert this ratio.

I run 6 automated agents in production. The prompt engineering took maybe 10% of the build time. The other 90% was the unglamorous work that keeps them alive.

Persistent state management so agents remember context across runs. Error handling for when APIs flake at 2am. Scheduling and orchestration so agents don't step on each other. Observability so you can audit what happened after the fact.

Here's my recommended stack for anyone going down this path:

First, build your state layer. Agents without memory between runs are just expensive cron jobs. Use a database, not files.

Second, implement circuit breakers. When an upstream API goes down, your agent should degrade gracefully, not produce garbage output.

Third, log everything. You need to reconstruct exactly what your agent did and why. This isn't optional. It's the difference between a demo and a system.

The teams I see succeeding with agents aren't the ones with the cleverest prompts. They're the ones who treat agent infrastructure like any other production system. With monitoring, alerting, rollback capability, and proper error handling.

Start with the plumbing. The prompts are the easy part.

The entire AI agent discourse is broken.

Scroll LinkedIn right now. It's wall-to-wall prompt engineering tips, system message templates, and "10x your agents with this one trick" posts. Meanwhile, the people actually running agents in production are wrestling with retry logic and cron failures at 2am.

I run 6 agents. Want to know my dirty secret? The prompts are boring. Simple instructions. Nothing clever. The engineering that matters is everything around the prompt.

State management. Because an agent that forgets what it did yesterday is useless.

Error handling. Because the Anthropic API will 529 on you at the worst possible moment and your agent needs to not light itself on fire when that happens.

Scheduling. Because running 6 agents on overlapping schedules with shared resources is a concurrency problem, not a prompt problem.

But nobody posts about their retry logic on LinkedIn. Nobody gets engagement from "here's my error handling middleware." So the discourse stays stuck on prompts while production systems stay brittle.

The prompt engineering era peaked. The agent infrastructure era is just starting. The people who figure out the plumbing will run circles around the people still tweaking their system messages.

posts.title: gtm stack as a codebase
posts.hook: Your GTM stack should be version controlled. Not in a spreadsheet. Not in someone's head. In a repo.
posts.body:
Your GTM stack should be version controlled. Not in a spreadsheet. Not in someone's head. In a repo.

I manage our entire go-to-market operation as code. Enrichment logic, scoring models, outbound sequences, CRM field mappings. All of it lives in files I can diff, review, and roll back.

Why this matters:

When a Clay enrichment breaks, I check the git log. When a scoring model drifts, I compare it to last month's version. When someone asks "why did we change the ICP definition," the commit message tells them.

Traditional rev ops teams document changes in Confluence pages nobody reads. Or worse, they don't document them at all. Someone adjusts a Salesforce workflow on a Friday afternoon and three weeks later pipeline numbers look weird and nobody knows why.

The GTM engineering approach treats your revenue system like software. Because it is software. It's just software that most teams build without any of the practices that make software reliable.

You don't need to be a developer to do this. Start with one thing. Put your ICP scoring criteria in a markdown file. Track changes. Review them quarterly. That alone puts you ahead of 90% of revenue teams.
posts.cta: What part of your GTM stack would benefit most from version control?

inspired_by.author: Sami Rahman
inspired_by.summary: Discusses building scalable GTM engines by integrating engineering principles into go-to-market strategies. Shares a framework for aligning product, sales, and marketing through data-driven automation. Emphasizes measuring GTM velocity as a key metric for revenue growth.

remixes.label: spreadsheet-graveyard
remixes.hook: Somewhere in your company, a critical GTM decision is being made based on a spreadsheet that was last updated by someone who left 6 months ago.
remixes.body:
Somewhere in your company, a critical GTM decision is being made based on a spreadsheet that was last updated by someone who left 6 months ago.

I've seen this pattern at every company I've worked with. The scoring model lives in a Google Sheet. The enrichment logic is documented in a Notion page that's 3 versions behind reality. The CRM field mappings exist only in one person's head.

So I did something different. I moved our entire GTM operation into a git repo.

Enrichment logic. Scoring models. Outbound sequences. CRM mappings. ICP definitions. All of it version controlled.

The results were immediate.

When a Clay enrichment broke last week, I didn't dig through Slack threads. I checked the git log, found the commit that changed the field mapping, and reverted it. Five minutes.

When our scoring model started flagging the wrong accounts, I diffed the current version against last month's. Found the threshold change that caused the drift. Fixed it with a one-line commit.

When a new team member joined, they didn't shadow someone for a week. They read the repo. Every decision, every change, every rollback documented in commit history.

Your GTM stack is a system. Treat it like one. Version control isn't just for code anymore.
remixes.cta: What's the most fragile part of your GTM operation right now? The thing that would break if one person left?

remixes.label: git-blame-for-revenue
remixes.hook: I can git blame every GTM decision we've made in the last 6 months. Can you?
remixes.body:
I can git blame every GTM decision we've made in the last 6 months. Can you?

Think about that for a second. Every scoring model change. Every enrichment rule update. Every ICP shift. Every sequence modification. All traceable to a specific commit, with context about why it changed.

This isn't theoretical. It's how I actually run our go-to-market operation.

Last month, our outbound reply rate dropped 30%. Instead of guessing, I ran a diff. Found that two weeks prior, we'd changed our enrichment threshold. The new threshold was letting through lower-quality accounts. One revert, reply rates recovered.

Total time to diagnose and fix: 8 minutes.

Compare that to the typical RevOps debugging process. "Something changed." "When?" "We're not sure." "What changed?" "Let me check with Sarah. She might have updated the scoring model. Or maybe it was the new CRM fields. Or the sequence changes."

That investigation takes days. Sometimes weeks. Sometimes you never figure it out.

Git gives you a time machine for your GTM decisions. You can see exactly what changed, when, who did it, and why. You can revert bad changes instantly. You can experiment knowing you can always roll back.

RevOps without version control is just hoping nothing breaks.
remixes.cta: How long does it take your team to figure out what changed when a GTM metric shifts unexpectedly?

If I could recommend one operational change to any RevOps team, it would be this: put your GTM stack under version control.

I manage our entire go-to-market operation as code. Enrichment logic, scoring models, outbound sequences, CRM field mappings. All of it lives in files I can diff, review, and roll back.

Here's why this matters and how to start:

First, the diagnostic advantage. When a Clay enrichment breaks, I check the git log. When a scoring model drifts, I compare it to last month's version. Every change is traceable. Every decision has context.

Second, the onboarding advantage. New team members read the repo instead of shadowing someone for a week. The commit history tells the story of every GTM decision.

Third, the experimentation advantage. You can branch, test new scoring models or enrichment approaches, and merge or revert based on results. No more "we changed something and can't undo it."

My recommended starting point: take your ICP definition and scoring model. Move them from whatever spreadsheet or doc they live in now into a structured file format. YAML or JSON. Commit it to a repo. Start tracking changes.

You don't need to move everything at once. Start with the pieces that break most often. Build the habit. Expand from there.

The ROI shows up the first time something breaks and you can diagnose it in minutes instead of days.

I moved our entire go-to-market operation into a git repo. Enrichment logic, scoring models, sequences, CRM mappings. All of it. And honestly, going back to the old way would feel like giving up electricity.

Because here's what nobody in RevOps wants to admit: most teams have zero idea what changed when metrics shift. None. They guess. They blame the market. They blame the data. They run a retrospective that concludes "we need better documentation" and then nothing changes.

Meanwhile, when my outbound reply rate dipped last month, I ran git diff and found the exact commit that changed our enrichment threshold. Fixed it in 8 minutes. Not 8 days. Not 8 meetings.

The RevOps community talks endlessly about tools. About Clay workflows and Apollo filters and HubSpot automations. But the meta-layer, how you track, version, and audit the decisions that configure those tools, gets zero attention.

You wouldn't deploy application code without version control. Why are you deploying revenue operations without it?

The teams that treat GTM like a codebase will outmaneuver the ones still living in spreadsheet chaos. That gap is only widening.

posts.title: shipping velocity with ai agents
posts.hook: I shipped more in the last 30 days than I did in Q4 2025. The difference wasn't working harder. It was working with agents.
posts.body:
I shipped more in the last 30 days than I did in Q4 2025. The difference wasn't working harder. It was working with agents.

My workflow now:

I describe what I want to build. Claude Code writes the first draft. I review, adjust, ship. Then I move to the next thing while an agent runs tests in the background.

The mental model shift is subtle but massive. I went from "how do I implement this" to "how do I describe this clearly enough that it gets implemented correctly." My job became specification and review, not keystroke production.

Some things that shipped this way:

➡️ A full blog post generator that runs on cron every night
➡️ An ABM pipeline that enriches and scores accounts automatically
➡️ A CRM integration that syncs data across three tools
➡️ Landing page generation for prospects in under 10 minutes

None of these are rocket science individually. But doing all of them in a month as a solo operator? That's the leverage.

The solo builder era isn't about being smarter. It's about having a multiplier on execution speed that didn't exist 18 months ago.

The founders shipping fastest right now aren't the best coders. They're the best specifiers.
posts.cta: What's the biggest thing you've shipped with AI assistance that would've taken 5x longer without it?

remixes.label: delegation-mindset-shift
remixes.hook: The hardest part of working with AI agents wasn't learning prompts. It was learning to let go of implementation.
remixes.body:
The hardest part of working with AI agents wasn't learning prompts. It was learning to let go of implementation.

For 15 years, my value was in how I implemented things. The elegant solution. The clever optimization. The code I could be proud of.

Then I started working with Claude Code daily, and I had to rewire my brain.

The shift: I stopped being the implementer and started being the architect.

I describe the system I want. The agent writes the first pass. I review it like I'd review a junior developer's PR. Push back on the parts that don't meet my standards. Accept the parts that work even if I would have written them differently.

That last part is the hard one. Accepting "different but functional" instead of insisting on "exactly how I'd do it."

The results speak for themselves. In the last 30 days, I shipped a full mission control dashboard, three automated content pipelines, an ABM enrichment engine, and a daily reporting system. All while running client work.

Previously, that would have been a quarter of work. Maybe two quarters.

But the velocity only works because I stopped trying to write every line myself. I focused on the decisions that actually matter. Architecture. Data flow. System boundaries. Error handling strategy.

The implementation details? Those are increasingly a commodity. The thinking that guides them is not.
remixes.cta: What's been the hardest mental shift for you in adopting AI-assisted development?

remixes.label: before-after-receipts
remixes.hook: Q4 2025: 2 features shipped per month. February 2026: 14 features shipped. Same person. Same hours. Different workflow.
remixes.body:
Q4 2025: 2 features shipped per month. February 2026: 14 features shipped. Same person. Same hours. Different workflow.

I want to be specific about what changed because "I use AI now" isn't useful advice.

The old workflow:
Think about what to build. Open editor. Write code line by line. Debug. Test. Fix. Repeat. Context switch to the next thing. Lose 20 minutes getting back into flow.

The new workflow:
Describe the feature in plain English with specific technical constraints. Agent writes the first draft. I review the diff, not the code. Push back on architectural choices. Accept implementation details that work. Ship. Move immediately to the next thing.

The key differences:

I never start from a blank file anymore. The agent's first draft might be 60% right, but 60% of a first draft in 2 minutes beats 0% after 45 minutes of setup.

I review in batches. While one agent runs tests, I'm describing the next feature to another session. Parallelism I couldn't achieve alone.

I focus on decisions, not keystrokes. "Should this be a webhook or polling?" matters. "How do I write the fetch call?" doesn't anymore.

The hours didn't change. The leverage did.

If you want to increase your shipping velocity with AI agents, here's the framework I'd recommend based on my own transition.

I shipped more in the last 30 days than I did in Q4 2025. The difference wasn't working harder. It was restructuring my workflow around agent-assisted development.

Here's how the workflow operates:

I describe what I want to build with specific technical constraints. Claude Code writes the first draft. I review the diff, adjust architecture-level decisions, and ship. Then I move to the next thing while an agent runs tests in the background.

The mental model shift is critical. I went from "how do I implement this" to "what's the right architecture for this, and does the agent's implementation match my intent."

Three things I'd recommend for anyone making this transition:

First, stop starting from blank files. Let the agent generate the scaffold. Even a mediocre first draft saves 30 minutes of boilerplate.

Second, review at the architecture level, not the syntax level. If the data flow is right and the error handling is sound, don't nitpick variable names.

Third, parallelize. While waiting for tests to pass in one session, start the next feature in another. Your bottleneck should be decision-making, not implementation.

The velocity gains are real, but they require letting go of the idea that you need to write every line yourself.
tones.provocateur:
Here's an uncomfortable truth for senior developers: your implementation skills are becoming commoditized faster than you think.

I shipped more in the last 30 days than I did in Q4 2025. Not because I got faster at coding. Because I mostly stopped coding.

My workflow now: I describe what I want. An agent writes it. I review it like a tech lead reviewing a PR. Ship. Next thing.

And I can already hear the objections. "AI-generated code is sloppy." Sometimes. That's what review is for. "You lose the deep understanding." I understand architecture, data flow, and system design. The agent handles the boilerplate.

"Real engineers write their own code." Real engineers ship working systems. The tool doesn't matter.

The developers who will thrive aren't the fastest typists. They're the ones who can decompose problems, make architectural decisions quickly, and review generated code with experienced eyes.

I've watched developers spend 3 hours hand-crafting a migration script that an agent writes in 90 seconds. That's not craftsmanship. That's stubbornness wearing a pride costume.

The velocity gap between agent-assisted developers and traditional developers is already 3-5x in my experience. By the end of this year, it'll be 10x.

You can debate whether that's good or bad. Or you can adapt your workflow and start shipping.

posts.title: agents wont replace devs
posts.hook:
"AI agents will handle 50% of dev tasks soon."

I think this framing misses the point entirely.
posts.body:
"AI agents will handle 50% of dev tasks soon."

I think this framing misses the point entirely.

Agents won't replace 50% of developers. They'll eliminate 50% of the process tax that slows developers down.

Boilerplate. Config files. Test scaffolding. Migration scripts. Documentation updates. The stuff that takes 2 hours but requires 5 minutes of actual thinking.

I watch this happen in real time. The creative decisions, the architecture choices, the "should we even build this" conversations... agents don't touch those. They shouldn't.

What agents are genuinely great at: taking a clear decision and executing the tedious implementation. Writing the 47 lines of TypeScript that wire up an API endpoint after you've decided what the endpoint should do.

The developers who'll struggle aren't the ones who "can't code." They're the ones whose entire value was typing speed and syntax memorization. If your contribution to a team is that you remember React hooks better than your coworkers, yeah, that's getting automated.

But if your contribution is knowing which problems to solve and how to decompose them... you just got a massive productivity boost.

The job isn't going away. The boring parts of the job are going away. That's different.
posts.cta: What's the most tedious dev task you've successfully offloaded to an agent?

remixes.label: process-tax-calculator
remixes.hook: I tracked how I spent my dev time for two weeks. 62% of it was process tax. Not thinking. Not designing. Just... plumbing.
remixes.body:
I tracked how I spent my dev time for two weeks. 62% of it was process tax. Not thinking. Not designing. Just plumbing.

Config files. Test boilerplate. Migration scripts. Documentation updates. Deployment checklists. The work that has to happen but requires almost zero creative thinking.

So I started offloading it to agents. Methodically. One category at a time.

First went the test scaffolding. Agent generates the test file structure, I fill in the actual assertions. Saved about 40 minutes per feature.

Then migration scripts. Describe the schema change, agent writes the migration. I review the SQL. Saved another 30 minutes per change.

Then documentation. Agent reads the diff, updates the relevant docs. I review for accuracy. This one was the biggest win because I used to just skip docs when I was busy.

The result: that 62% process tax dropped to roughly 25%. Not zero, because I still review everything. But the active thinking time went from 38% to about 75% of my day.

That's the real story about agents and developers. Nobody's getting replaced. The process tax is getting eliminated. And developers who used to spend most of their day on mechanical work are suddenly spending most of it on actual problem-solving.

That's not a threat to developers. That's the best thing that's happened to the profession in a decade.
remixes.cta: What percentage of your dev time would you estimate is process tax vs. actual creative problem-solving?

remixes.label: 5-minutes-of-thinking
remixes.hook: Most dev tasks that take 2 hours only contain about 5 minutes of actual thinking. Agents are coming for the other 115 minutes.
remixes.body:
Most dev tasks that take 2 hours only contain about 5 minutes of actual thinking. Agents are coming for the other 115 minutes.

Break down any typical development task. Say, adding a new API endpoint.

The thinking: What should the endpoint do? What data does it need? What are the edge cases? What's the error handling strategy? 5 minutes of real decisions.

The mechanical work: Create the route file. Write the handler boilerplate. Add input validation. Write the database query. Create the migration. Add tests. Update the API docs. Update the types. 115 minutes of typing things you already know.

I'm not being dismissive of the mechanical work. It has to be correct. But it doesn't require invention. It requires execution of known patterns.

That's exactly what agents are good at. Pattern execution.

So the future isn't "agents replace developers." It's developers who spend 5% of their time thinking and 95% typing becoming developers who spend 40% of their time thinking and 60% directing agents.

The thinking was always the valuable part. We just couldn't separate it from the typing before.

Now we can. And the developers who lean into this, who get really good at the 5-minute thinking part, will build things at a pace that seems impossible by today's standards.

The mechanical work was never the craft. The decisions were.
remixes.cta: Think about your last dev task. How many minutes were actual decisions vs. executing known patterns?

tones.builder:
"AI agents will handle 50% of dev tasks soon."

I think this framing misses the point entirely.

Agents won't replace 50% of developers. They'll eliminate 50% of the process tax that slows developers down.

Boilerplate. Config files. Test scaffolding. Migration scripts. Documentation updates. The stuff that takes 2 hours but requires 5 minutes of actual thinking.

I watch this happen in real time. The creative decisions, the architecture choices, the "should we even build this" conversations... agents don't touch those. They shouldn't.

What agents are genuinely great at: taking a clear decision and executing the tedious implementation. Writing the 47 lines of TypeScript that wire up an API endpoint after you've decided what the endpoint should do.

The developers who'll struggle aren't the ones who "can't code." They're the ones whose entire value was typing speed and syntax memorization. If your contribution to a team is that you remember React hooks better than your coworkers, yeah, that's getting automated.

But if your contribution is knowing which problems to solve and how to decompose them... you just got a massive productivity boost.

The job isn't going away. The boring parts of the job are going away. That's different.
tones.advisor:
I'd push back on the common narrative that AI agents will replace developers. The more accurate framing: agents will eliminate the process tax that slows developers down.

Boilerplate. Config files. Test scaffolding. Migration scripts. Documentation updates. The stuff that takes 2 hours but requires 5 minutes of actual thinking.

I watch this happen in real time in my own workflow. Here's what I'd recommend for teams evaluating where agents fit:

Start by auditing your process tax. Track how developers spend their time for two weeks. Categorize each task as "creative thinking" or "pattern execution." Most teams find 50-70% of dev time is pattern execution.

Then offload the pattern execution systematically. Test scaffolding first, because it's low-risk and high-volume. Then boilerplate generation. Then documentation. Each one frees up developer time for the work that actually requires human judgment.

The developers who will succeed in this environment are the ones who sharpen their architectural thinking, their ability to decompose problems, and their code review skills. The typing speed and boilerplate memorization that used to matter are becoming less relevant each month.

My recommendation: don't fear the shift. Invest in the skills that agents can't replicate. System design. Trade-off analysis. Understanding user needs. Those become more valuable, not less, as mechanical coding gets automated.
tones.provocateur:
"AI agents will handle 50% of dev tasks soon."

I think this framing misses the point entirely. And it's creating panic where there should be excitement.

Agents won't replace 50% of developers. They'll eliminate 50% of the process tax that makes developers miserable.

Be honest. Do you enjoy writing test boilerplate? Config files? Migration scripts? Documentation updates? That stuff takes 2 hours and requires 5 minutes of actual thinking. It's not craft. It's clerical work wearing a developer costume.

And yet the industry treats these mechanical tasks as evidence of "real engineering." Hand-write your tests or you're not a real developer. Type out every migration or you don't understand your schema. Write the docs yourself or you won't appreciate the API.

That's hazing, not engineering.

The developers I know who've embraced agents aren't getting dumber. They're getting sharper. Because they're spending their mental energy on architecture and system design instead of burning it on boilerplate.

The fear that agents will replace developers comes from people who've confused typing with thinking. If your job is mostly typing known patterns, then yes, you should be concerned. But if your job is making decisions about systems, you just got the biggest leverage upgrade of your career.

Stop mourning the boilerplate. Start building things that matter.

posts.title: outbound isnt dead lazy outbound is
posts.hook:
Cold outbound isn't dying. Lazy outbound is.

The difference is about 45 minutes of enrichment work per account.
posts.body:
Cold outbound isn't dying. Lazy outbound is.

The difference is about 45 minutes of enrichment work per account.

I used to send 200+ emails a day from primary domains with zero warmup on SalesLoft. Most of them were trash. The hit rate was terrible and I knew it while clicking send.

Now I watch teams automate that same bad approach at 10x scale and wonder why their domain reputation craters in a week.

The fix isn't "stop doing outbound." The fix is doing the enrichment work before you write the first email.

🔧 Pull their tech stack from BuiltWith
🔧 Check job postings for signals they're hiring for the problem you solve
🔧 Find their recent funding round and map it to expansion pain points
🔧 Cross-reference their CRM activity with your ICP scoring

Then write one email that proves you did the work. Not a template with {first_name} and {company_name} swapped in. An actual message about their actual situation.

The irony is that AI makes both approaches easier. You can use it to blast generic messages faster. Or you can use it to do deep account research in minutes instead of hours.

Same tool. Completely different outcomes. The tool doesn't decide. The operator does.
posts.cta: What's your minimum enrichment threshold before you'll send a cold email?

remixes.label: domain-reputation-autopsy
remixes.hook: I watched a company burn their primary domain in 3 weeks. 200 cold emails a day. No warmup. No enrichment. It was like watching a slow-motion car crash.
remixes.body:
I watched a company burn their primary domain in 3 weeks. 200 cold emails a day. No warmup. No enrichment. It was like watching a slow-motion car crash.

The playbook was simple and wrong: scrape a list, write a generic template, blast it to everyone, pray for replies. When replies didn't come, send more. When domain reputation tanked, buy a new domain. Repeat.

This was standard practice 5 years ago. It barely worked then. It absolutely doesn't work now.

Here's what changed: email providers got smarter. Google and Microsoft actively penalize bulk senders with low engagement. Your domain reputation is a finite resource, and every bad email burns it down.

So what actually works now?

Enrichment before everything. I spend 30-45 minutes per account before a single email goes out. Technographics, hiring signals, funding data, recent content they've published. Not because I'm thorough for fun. Because a relevant email to 20 accounts outperforms a generic email to 2,000.

Domain warming as infrastructure. Separate sending domains. Gradual volume ramps. Engagement tracking per domain. Treat it like a production system, not a disposable resource.

Quality signals in every message. Reference something specific. Show you did the work. Give the recipient a reason to believe this isn't the 47th identical email they got today.

The math is simple: 20 enriched emails with a 15% reply rate beats 2,000 spray-and-pray emails with a 0.3% reply rate. And your domain lives to send another day.
remixes.cta: What's the minimum research you do per account before you'll hit send on a cold email?

remixes.label: enrichment-as-competitive-moat
remixes.hook: Your competitors are still sending 500 generic cold emails a day. That's not your problem. That's your advantage.
remixes.body:
Your competitors are still sending 500 generic cold emails a day. That's not your problem. That's your advantage.

Every lazy mass email they send trains the market to ignore outbound. It makes recipients more skeptical, more guarded, more likely to delete anything that looks templated.

Which means the bar for standing out is lower than you think. You just have to clear it.

I built an enrichment pipeline that spends about 45 minutes of compute per account before generating any outreach. Here's what it collects:

Technographic signals. What tools they use, what they've recently adopted or dropped.

Hiring patterns. What roles they're filling tells you where they're investing and where they have pain.

Content signals. What their leadership is posting about publicly. Their priorities, stated in their own words.

Timing indicators. Funding rounds, leadership changes, product launches. Moments when they're most likely to be evaluating new solutions.

All of this feeds into a personalization layer that generates outreach specific to each account. Not "Hi [First Name], I noticed your company does [Industry]." Real specificity. Real relevance.

The result: 12-18% reply rates on cold outbound. In a world where most teams celebrate 2%.

Lazy outbound is dying because the market adapted. But that adaptation created a massive opening for anyone willing to do the enrichment work. The bar is on the floor. Step over it.
remixes.cta: What enrichment signal has been most predictive of a positive reply in your outbound?

Cold outbound isn't dying. Lazy outbound is. And the difference comes down to enrichment discipline.

I used to send 200+ emails a day from primary domains with zero warmup. Most were trash. The hit rate was terrible and I knew it while clicking send.

Here's what I'd recommend for any team running outbound in 2026:

First, establish a minimum enrichment threshold. I recommend 45 minutes of research per account before any outreach. Technographics, hiring signals, funding data, and recent content from their leadership. This sounds slow until you compare the math: 20 enriched emails at 15% reply rate vs. 2,000 generic emails at 0.3%.

Second, treat domain reputation as infrastructure. Separate sending domains from your primary. Warm them gradually. Monitor engagement metrics per domain. When engagement drops below your threshold, pause and diagnose before sending more.

Third, build quality signals into every message. Reference something specific about their business. Connect your value prop to a real initiative they're pursuing. Give them evidence that this email was written for them, not batch-generated.

The teams I see winning at outbound right now aren't the ones with the biggest lists or the most sophisticated automation. They're the ones with the most disciplined enrichment process. Volume is easy to scale. Relevance is the hard part, and it's where the results live.

posts.title: gtm engineering is the new full stack
posts.hook: Three years ago "GTM engineer" wasn't a job title. Now it's the most interesting role in B2B.
posts.body:
Three years ago "GTM engineer" wasn't a job title. Now it's the most interesting role in B2B.

Revenue teams have more tools than ever. Clay, Apollo, Attio, Instantly, PostHog, Supabase... the list keeps growing. But the people buying these tools can't wire them together. And the engineers who could wire them together don't understand pipeline mechanics.

GTM engineering fills that gap. You need someone who knows what a buying committee looks like AND can write the API integration that maps it automatically.

The best GTM engineers share a pattern. They started in revenue roles. SDR, AE, rev ops. They hit the ceiling of what manual work could accomplish. Then they learned to code. Not to become software engineers, but to automate their own job.

That progression matters. You can't automate a workflow you've never done by hand. You won't catch edge cases in a scoring model if you've never manually qualified a lead.

The stack is converging. Data enrichment, outbound automation, CRM orchestration, content distribution, analytics. It's all becoming one system. And the people who can build that system end-to-end are commanding serious leverage.

This isn't a trend. It's a structural shift in how B2B companies operate.
posts.cta: Are you seeing GTM engineering roles show up at your company? What does the scope look like?

remixes.label: hiring-signal-decode
remixes.hook: I just searched "GTM engineer" on LinkedIn jobs. 1,200+ open roles. Two years ago? Under 50.
remixes.body:
I just searched "GTM engineer" on LinkedIn jobs. 1,200+ open roles. Two years ago? Under 50.

Here's what's driving it. Revenue leaders realized they're sitting on 15+ tools and zero integration between them. Clay pulls enrichment data. Apollo sequences outbound. Attio tracks pipeline. PostHog captures product signals. But none of these tools talk to each other out of the box.

So companies started hiring. Not traditional ops people. Not pure software engineers. Something in between.

The job descriptions tell the story. You'll see requirements like: "Build automated enrichment pipelines." "Design lead scoring models from product usage data." "Own the technical architecture of our outbound stack."

This is engineering work applied to revenue problems. And it pays like engineering work too. Senior GTM engineer roles are posting at $180-220K. That's not ops money. That's software money.

The career path is interesting because it pulls from three worlds. You need enough engineering skill to build real systems. Enough revenue context to know what matters to pipeline. And enough data literacy to measure what's working.

If you're an engineer bored by pure product work, or a rev ops person who keeps hitting the ceiling of no-code tools, this is the role to watch.
remixes.cta: Have you seen GTM engineer postings cross your feed? What salary ranges are you seeing?

remixes.label: death-of-the-ops-generalist
remixes.hook: The rev ops generalist is being replaced. Not by AI. By engineers who understand pipeline.
remixes.body:
The rev ops generalist is being replaced. Not by AI. By engineers who understand pipeline.

For years, revenue operations meant configuring Salesforce, building dashboards, and managing a Zapier spaghetti mess. The job was tool administration. Click here, map this field, pray the sync doesn't break.

That era is ending. The new GTM stack is programmable. APIs everywhere. Webhook-driven workflows. Infrastructure-as-code applied to go-to-market.

The people thriving in this new world write Python scripts to orchestrate Clay tables. They build custom scoring models in Supabase instead of relying on vendor black boxes. They treat the CRM as a database, not a UI.

I made this transition myself. Went from traditional consulting to building automated ABM systems. The shift wasn't about learning one new tool. It was about thinking in systems instead of sequences.

 for ops professionals: if your primary skill is configuring vendor tools through their UI, you're competing with every other admin who took the same certification course. If you can write the code that connects those tools, you're suddenly scarce.

GTM engineering isn't a trendy rebrand. It's what happens when revenue infrastructure gets complex enough to require real engineering.

posts.title: multi-agent orchestration lessons
posts.hook: I run multi-agent systems in production. The biggest lesson? Agents are terrible at coordinating with each other unless you architect it.
posts.body:
I run multi-agent systems in production. The biggest lesson? Agents are terrible at coordinating with each other unless you architect it.

Three patterns that actually work:

1. Hub and spoke, not peer-to-peer

Don't let agents talk to each other directly. One orchestrator agent delegates tasks, collects results, decides next steps. Peer-to-peer agent communication turns into chaos fast.

2. Explicit handoff contracts

Every agent writes its output to a defined schema. The next agent in the chain knows exactly what format to expect. No "figure it out from context." Structured data in, structured data out.

3. Kill switches everywhere

Agents will loop. They'll retry failed operations indefinitely. They'll spend $50 on API calls chasing an edge case that doesn't matter. Every agent needs a max iteration count, a cost ceiling, and a timeout.

The multi-agent hype is real but messy. Most demos show two agents having a conversation. Production multi-agent systems look more like microservices. Clear boundaries, defined interfaces, independent failure modes.

If you're building multi-agent workflows, think less like a prompt engineer and more like a systems architect. The coordination layer is harder than any individual agent's task.

remixes.hook: My first multi-agent system failed spectacularly. Three agents, zero coordination, a CRM full of duplicate records.
remixes.body:
My first multi-agent system failed spectacularly. Three agents, zero coordination, a CRM full of duplicate records.

I had a research agent pulling company data, an enrichment agent cross-referencing it, and a scoring agent evaluating fit. Seemed clean on the whiteboard. In practice, the research agent would update a record while the enrichment agent was reading it. The scoring agent would score stale data. Race conditions everywhere.

The fix wasn't better prompts. It was better architecture.

I moved to an explicit state machine. Each record has a status: raw, researched, enriched, scored. Agents only pick up records in their designated state. When they finish, they advance the state and move on. No agent ever touches another agent's work-in-progress.

This sounds obvious in hindsight. But the temptation with agents is to let them be "smart" and figure it out. They won't. They need hard boundaries.

Second lesson: logging everything is non-negotiable. When an agent makes a decision, it writes a structured log entry explaining what it did and why. Without this, debugging multi-agent systems is like debugging distributed systems without traces. Which is exactly what it is.

Third lesson: timeouts and dead letter queues. Agents hang. They hallucinate and get stuck in loops. You need a watchdog process that kills stuck work and routes it to a human review queue.

Multi-agent systems work. But only when you treat them as distributed systems, not magic.
remixes.cta: What was your worst multi-agent failure? What did the debugging process look like?

remixes.label: single-vs-multi-decision
remixes.hook: You probably don't need multi-agent orchestration. Here's how I decide when one agent is enough.
remixes.body:
You probably don't need multi-agent orchestration. Here's how I decide when one agent is enough.

The honest answer: most tasks that people build multi-agent systems for could be handled by one well-prompted agent with good tool access. Multi-agent adds complexity. Latency. Cost. Debugging difficulty. You should have a clear reason for it.

I use multi-agent when exactly two conditions are met. First, the task has genuinely independent subtasks that benefit from parallel execution. Second, the subtasks require different tool access or context windows.

Example where multi-agent wins: researching 50 companies simultaneously. Each research task is independent. Parallelism saves hours. One orchestrator delegates, 50 workers execute, results aggregate.

Example where single-agent wins: writing and refining a sales email. This is a sequential creative task. Adding more agents doesn't help. It just creates committee-designed output.

The architecture decision tree I use:

Can one agent do this in a reasonable time? Use one agent.
Are there independent subtasks? Consider parallel workers with an orchestrator.
Do subtasks need different tools or permissions? Multi-agent with role isolation.
Is there a feedback loop between subtasks? Keep it in one agent or use explicit message passing.

The goal isn't to use the most agents. It's to use the fewest agents that get the job done reliably.
remixes.cta: How do you decide when to split work across agents vs. keeping it in a single agent?

tones.builder:
I run multi-agent systems in production. The biggest lesson? Agents are terrible at coordinating with each other unless you architect it.

Three patterns that actually work:

1. Hub and spoke, not peer-to-peer

Don't let agents talk to each other directly. One orchestrator agent delegates tasks, collects results, decides next steps. Peer-to-peer agent communication turns into chaos fast.

2. Explicit handoff contracts

Every agent writes its output to a defined schema. The next agent in the chain knows exactly what format to expect. No "figure it out from context." Structured data in, structured data out.

3. Kill switches everywhere

Agents will loop. They'll retry failed operations indefinitely. They'll spend $50 on API calls chasing an edge case that doesn't matter. Every agent needs a max iteration count, a cost ceiling, and a timeout.

The multi-agent hype is real but messy. Most demos show two agents having a conversation. Production multi-agent systems look more like microservices. Clear boundaries, defined interfaces, independent failure modes.

If you're building multi-agent workflows, think less like a prompt engineer and more like a systems architect. The coordination layer is harder than any individual agent's task.
tones.advisor:
I run multi-agent systems in production. The biggest lesson? Agents are terrible at coordinating with each other unless you architect it.

If you're evaluating multi-agent architectures, here are the three patterns I recommend based on what I've seen work at scale.

First, use hub and spoke, not peer-to-peer. One orchestrator agent delegates tasks, collects results, and decides next steps. I've seen teams try peer-to-peer agent communication. It turns into chaos within days. The orchestrator pattern gives you a single point of control and a clear audit trail.

Second, implement explicit handoff protocols. Every agent should know exactly what inputs it receives and what outputs it produces. Define schemas for inter-agent communication. Treat it like an API contract. When Agent A finishes research, it writes a structured payload that Agent B consumes. No ambiguity. No implicit assumptions.

Third, build observability from day one. Log every agent decision, every tool call, every state transition. When something goes wrong in a multi-agent system, and it will, you need full traceability. I use structured JSON logs with correlation IDs that let me reconstruct the entire decision chain.

The pattern I'd caution against: giving agents the ability to spawn other agents dynamically. It sounds powerful. In practice, it creates unpredictable cost and latency spikes. Keep your agent topology static and well-defined.
tones.provocateur:
I run multi-agent systems in production. The biggest lesson? Agents are terrible at coordinating with each other unless you architect it.

But Twitter keeps telling you to build "swarms." Just throw agents at the problem. Let them figure it out. Emergent intelligence.

That's not engineering. That's hope.

Here's what actually happens when you let agents coordinate freely. Agent A calls Agent B with a vague request. Agent B interprets it differently than intended. Agent B calls Agent C for clarification. Agent C hallucinates an answer. Agent B acts on the hallucination. Agent A gets garbage back and retries. You've burned $4 in API calls and 90 seconds to accomplish nothing.

The agents-as-autonomous-peers mental model is wrong. The right model is agents-as-microservices. Defined interfaces. Explicit contracts. No direct communication between workers. Everything goes through the orchestrator.

I know this sounds boring. It is boring. That's the point. Boring architecture is reliable architecture.

The three patterns that work: hub-and-spoke routing, explicit state machines for handoffs, and structured logging for every decision. Nothing fancy. No emergent behavior. Just deterministic workflows where each agent has a clear job and a clear output format.

The people shipping real multi-agent systems don't talk about swarms. They talk about error handling, timeout policies, and dead letter queues. If that doesn't sound exciting to you, you're not ready for production agents.

posts.title: solo builder leverage is real
posts.hook: A solo builder in 2026 has more leverage than a 10-person startup did in 2020. This isn't hype. I'm living it.
posts.body:
A solo builder in 2026 has more leverage than a 10-person startup did in 2020. This isn't hype. I'm living it.

I run three websites, an automated content pipeline, a full ABM engine, CRM integrations, and a daily cron system that handles enrichment, scoring, and outreach. All from one Mac Mini.

No employees. No contractors. No DevOps team.

The stack that makes this possible:

➡️ Claude Code for building and shipping features
➡️ Cloudflare for hosting and edge compute
➡️ Cron jobs for anything that should run without me
➡️ SQLite for data that needs to persist
➡️ Python scripts for glue code between systems

The economics are absurd. My total infrastructure cost is less than one junior developer's monthly salary. And the system runs 24/7 whether I'm working or not.

But solo building isn't for everyone. The hard part isn't the tech. It's the context switching. You're the PM, the engineer, the designer, the ops person, and the sales team. All in the same afternoon.

The founders thriving in this era share one trait. They're comfortable being mediocre at many things instead of excellent at one thing. Breadth over depth. Ship over perfect.

The barrier to building has never been lower. The barrier to shipping consistently is exactly as high as it's always been.
posts.cta: What's the one tool or system that gives you the most solo builder leverage right now?

remixes.label: infrastructure-breakdown
remixes.hook: People ask me how I run a full GTM operation solo. Here's the boring answer: one Mac Mini and a lot of cron jobs.
remixes.body:
People ask me how I run a full GTM operation solo. Here's the boring answer: one Mac Mini and a lot of cron jobs.

No Kubernetes. No cloud fleet. No team of DevOps engineers. Just a single machine running scheduled tasks that would've required a department five years ago.

Midnight: a cron job pulls enrichment data from Apollo, scores accounts based on engagement signals, and updates the CRM. 6 AM: content gets generated, reviewed against quality gates, and queued for publishing. 10 PM: the ABM pipeline runs, matching ideal customer profiles against fresh firmographic data.

The whole system runs on about $200/month in API costs.

But here's the part people miss when they hear "solo builder." It's not about doing everything yourself. It's about building systems that do the work for you. I write the code once. The machines execute it thousands of times.

The real leverage isn't AI itself. It's AI plus automation plus infrastructure-as-code. Claude helps me write and debug the systems. Cron keeps them running. Git keeps them versioned. Structured logging keeps them debuggable.

I spent years in consulting managing teams that did manually what I now automate in a weekend. Not because I'm faster. Because the tools got that much better.

The uncomfortable question for every growing startup: are you hiring people to do work that a well-architected system could handle?
remixes.cta: What's the most impactful thing you've automated as a solo builder? What still requires a human touch?

remixes.label: contrarian-headcount
remixes.hook: Startups brag about headcount. I brag about keeping mine at one.
remixes.body:
Startups brag about headcount. I brag about keeping mine at one.

Every time I think about hiring, I ask a different question first: can I build a system that eliminates the need for this role?

Needed content? Built an automated pipeline that generates, quality-checks, and schedules posts. Needed lead enrichment? Built a cron that pulls firmographic data, scores it, and routes it to the CRM. Needed outbound? Built sequencing that personalizes based on real engagement signals.

Each of these would have been a full-time hire two years ago. Now they're scripts on a cron schedule.

I'm not anti-hiring. Some roles genuinely need humans. Strategic sales conversations. Complex client relationships. Creative direction. But a huge percentage of "jobs" in the modern startup are really just processes that haven't been automated yet.

The leverage math is wild. A solo builder with the right stack can cover:

Content marketing (automated pipeline)
Demand generation (ABM engine)
Sales ops (CRM integrations and enrichment)
DevOps (infrastructure-as-code on one machine)
Analytics (event tracking and dashboards)

That's five roles. Five salaries. Five sets of benefits. Five management relationships. Or one person and good architecture.

I'm not saying this to be smug. I'm saying it because the economics of small teams changed permanently in the last two years, and most people haven't updated their mental models.
remixes.cta: What role did you automate away instead of hiring for? How did it work out?

But here's the nuance people miss. The tools alone don't create leverage. What creates leverage is the decision to build systems instead of doing tasks. Every time I catch myself doing something manually more than twice, I stop and automate it. That discipline compounds over months.

The constraint isn't technical capability anymore. It's the mental shift from "I need to hire someone for this" to "I need to build something for this."

I know this take makes people uncomfortable. Nobody wants to hear that their department might be a script. But the math doesn't care about your feelings. And the solo builders who figured this out are eating the lunch of teams 10x their size.

posts.title: unified ops kills data fragmentation
posts.hook: Most companies have a sales ops team, a marketing ops team, and a rev ops team. All three are configuring the same CRM differently.
posts.body:
Most companies have a sales ops team, a marketing ops team, and a rev ops team. All three are configuring the same CRM differently.

This is how pipeline data gets messy.

I've seen companies where marketing marks a lead as "qualified" based on content engagement, sales marks the same lead as "unqualified" because they didn't respond to a cold call, and rev ops is running reports that blend both definitions without knowing it.

The fix isn't better reporting. The fix is one ops layer with one data model.

What that looks like in practice:

✅ Single source of truth for lead/account status definitions
✅ One scoring model that all teams reference, not three competing ones
✅ Enrichment happens once at ingestion, not re-enriched by each team's favorite tool
✅ Stage transitions are event-driven, not manually updated by humans who forget

The tech stack matters less than the architecture. You can do this in Salesforce, HubSpot, or Attio. The tool isn't the problem. The fragmentation is.

Smaller companies actually have an advantage here. Fewer people means fewer competing definitions. If you're under 50 people and your ops are already fragmented... fix it now before it calcifies.

Every month you wait, the cleanup gets exponentially harder.
posts.cta: What's the most painful data fragmentation issue you've dealt with across teams?

inspired_by.author: Dan Khan
inspired_by.summary: Explains how to unify sales ops and revenue operations for efficient scaling. Details tech stack recommendations for automation and dashboards that track cross-functional KPIs. Shares lessons from transforming a $50M ARR company's ops.

remixes.label: crm-civil-war
remixes.hook: Your CRM has three versions of the truth. Marketing's truth. Sales's truth. And whatever rev ops cobbled together last quarter.
remixes.body:
Your CRM has three versions of the truth. Marketing's truth. Sales's truth. And whatever rev ops cobbled together last quarter.

I've audited GTM stacks at companies of all sizes. The pattern is always the same. Marketing sets up lifecycle stages based on content engagement. Downloads a whitepaper? MQL. Sales sets up their own qualification criteria based on discovery calls. Had a meeting? SQL. Rev ops tries to report across both and gets numbers that don't match.

The root cause isn't bad people. It's bad architecture.

Each team configured the CRM to match their workflow instead of building a shared data model first. Now you've got three different definitions of "qualified," two conflicting scoring systems, and a board deck that requires manual data reconciliation every month.

The fix isn't another tool. It's one person (or one system) that owns the data model. Period.

Here's what that looks like in practice. Single source of truth for lifecycle stages. One scoring model that weighs both marketing signals and sales interactions. Shared definitions documented and enforced at the schema level, not the wiki level.

I built this for my own GTM stack. One CRM. One scoring model. One lifecycle definition. Automated enrichment writes to defined fields. Scoring reads from defined inputs. No team can override the schema.

Is it less flexible? Yes. Does it produce trustworthy data? Also yes. That trade-off is worth it every single time.
remixes.cta: How many conflicting definitions of "qualified lead" exist at your company right now?

remixes.label: cost-of-messy-data
remixes.hook: Messy pipeline data isn't an ops problem. It's a revenue problem that compounds quarterly.
remixes.body:
Messy pipeline data isn't an ops problem. It's a revenue problem that compounds quarterly.

Let me show you how it compounds. Q1: marketing and sales define "qualified" differently. Small discrepancy. Nobody notices. Q2: reports start diverging. Marketing says pipeline is healthy. Sales says it's garbage. Leadership isn't sure who to believe. Q3: someone builds a dashboard that tries to reconcile both. It introduces a third definition. Q4: the board asks why forecast accuracy dropped 20%. Everyone blames the CRM.

The CRM isn't the problem. The absence of a shared data contract is the problem.

In software engineering, teams solved this years ago. You define schemas. You enforce them with validation. You version them. You break the build if someone violates the contract. Revenue teams? Still using free-text fields and honor-system data entry.

The companies getting this right treat pipeline data like production code. Defined schemas for every object. Validation rules that reject bad data at the point of entry. Automated enrichment that fills gaps so humans don't have to guess. Audit logs that show who changed what and when.

This isn't over-engineering. This is applying basic data discipline to the system that determines your revenue forecast. If your finance team ran their books the way most companies run their CRM, they'd get fired.

The fix is boring but effective: one data model, enforced at the system level, owned by one team.
remixes.cta: When was the last time your pipeline data matched between marketing and sales reports?

posts.title: edge inference reshapes gtm
posts.hook: Running AI at the edge isn't just a developer tools story. It's about to change how GTM systems respond to buyers.
posts.body:
Running AI at the edge isn't just a developer tools story. It's about to change how GTM systems respond to buyers.

Right now most GTM automation works like this: visitor hits your site, event fires to analytics, batch job runs overnight, enrichment happens, lead gets scored, SDR sees it next morning. 12-18 hour lag minimum.

Edge inference compresses that to milliseconds.

Imagine: visitor lands on your pricing page. At the edge, before the page fully loads, an agent checks their company against your ICP, pulls enrichment data, scores the account, and personalizes the page content. By the time they scroll down, the case studies match their industry and the CTA reflects their company size.

This isn't theoretical. The compute layer exists today on Vercel, Cloudflare Workers, and similar platforms. The models are small enough. The inference is fast enough.

What's missing is GTM teams thinking this way. Most revenue teams still operate on batch processing mental models. Nightly syncs. Weekly reports. Monthly pipeline reviews.

The teams that figure out real-time GTM will have a structural advantage that's hard to replicate. Not because the technology is secret, but because the organizational shift from batch to real-time is genuinely hard.

The infrastructure is ready. The mental model hasn't caught up yet.
posts.cta: Is your GTM stack real-time or batch? What would change if you could react to buyer signals instantly?

remixes.label: speed-kills-deals
remixes.hook: Your competitor responded to that lead in 200 milliseconds. You responded the next morning. Guess who won.
remixes.body:
Your competitor responded to that lead in 200 milliseconds. You responded the next morning. Guess who won.

The response time gap in B2B is about to collapse. Here's why.

Most GTM stacks process buyer signals in batches. Someone visits your pricing page at 2 PM. The event fires to your analytics tool. A batch job runs overnight. Enrichment happens at 6 AM. The lead gets scored by 8 AM. Your SDR sees it at 9 AM. Nineteen hours later.

Edge inference changes the math. Instead of sending events to a cloud, processing them in batch, and routing them back, you run the model where the event happens. On the CDN. On the server handling the request. In the browser itself.

Visitor lands on your pricing page? The edge function identifies their company, checks your ICP criteria, scores them, and triggers a personalized response. All before the page finishes loading.

This isn't theoretical. The infrastructure exists today. Cloudflare Workers run inference at the edge. Vercel Edge Functions support lightweight models. Even browser-based models can run classification tasks.

The implications for GTM are massive. Real-time personalization based on firmographic data. Instant routing to the right sales rep. Dynamic pricing page content based on company size and industry. Chat experiences that already know who you are.

The companies that figure out real-time GTM will have an unfair advantage. Not because their product is better. Because their speed is better. And in B2B, speed is trust.

remixes.hook: Batch processing in GTM is the new technical debt. You just don't see it on the balance sheet.
remixes.body:
Batch processing in GTM is the new technical debt. You just don't see it on the balance sheet.

Every batch job in your pipeline is a delay. Every delay is an opportunity for a buyer to lose interest, find a competitor, or forget why they visited your site in the first place.

Think about what runs in batch today at most companies. Lead enrichment. Runs nightly. Lead scoring. Updates every 6 hours. List building. Weekly export from the data warehouse. Outbound personalization. Pulls from yesterday's data.

Now imagine all of that running in real time.

A prospect visits your site. Before the page loads, edge inference identifies them, pulls firmographic data, runs them through your ICP model, and decides: show them the enterprise pricing page, trigger a Slack alert to their assigned AE, and add them to a personalized email sequence. Total time: under one second.

This is where edge computing meets GTM. The models are small enough to run on CDN nodes. The data lookups are fast enough for real-time enrichment. The routing logic is simple enough for edge functions.

The shift from batch to real-time GTM will separate the next generation of fast-moving companies from the ones still running overnight cron jobs. I'm already building pieces of this into my own stack, moving enrichment and scoring closer to the event source.

The tooling isn't perfect yet. But the direction is clear. Real-time wins.
